metadata.title: DevProof: Prove Your Code. Build Your Credibility.
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.

tags: DevProof: Prove Your Code. Build Your Cr
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Dhruv Patel — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. DevProof turns a developer's work into demonstrated credibility, not just a list of issues. It runs a Next.js 16 frontend on Vercel and a FastAPI backend on Cloud Run. Its ranking weights relevance at 40%, freshness at 35%, and popularity at 25%. Demand arrived early, with people already asking to try the tool.

artifactLines: DevProof turns a developer's work into demonstrated credibility, not just a list of issues.
It runs a Next.js 16 frontend on Vercel and a FastAPI backend on Cloud Run.
Its ranking weights relevance at 40%, freshness at 35%, and popularity at 25%.
Demand arrived early, with people already asking to try the tool.

beats.narration_text: I like that DevProof outputs credibility rather than just a list of issues, and that its ranking blends relevance at 40%, freshness at 35%, and popularity at 25% on a Next.js 16 and FastAPI stack. I want to see how those weights shape results. Show me: (1) a worked example where changing freshness weight reorders two items; (2) why relevance gets the largest share; (3) how the Cloud Run backend and Vercel frontend split the ranking work.
